meta.title: March Madness
meta.author: Michael T. Foley
meta.focusQuestion: What makes March Madness so popular in the United States?

sections.title: Now or Never
paragraphs: The date is April 4, 1983. The entire country watches as the North Carolina (NC) State Wolfpack faces off against the heavily favored Houston Cougars in their national championship game. Underdogs NC State are playing the game of their lives, and with mere seconds left the score is tied 52-52. NC State guard Dereck Whittenburg has possession of the ball but is quickly running out of time. With the final seconds ticking away, Whittenburg has no choice but to launch the ball at the net and hope for the best. The shot falls short.

Suddenly, NC State teammate Lorenzo Charles comes out of nowhere to catch the ball in midair. He dunks it into the net just as the clock ticks to zero. The crowd erupts in chaos and celebration. It has just witnessed one of the biggest and most exciting upsets in college basketball history.
This is what March Madness is all about: excitement, high stakes, and unpredictability. With sixty-eight teams playing in a single-elimination tournament, anything can happen.

The National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) organizes, coordinates, and regulates college and university sports in the United States. On March 17, 1939, the NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Tournament began its historic run. Back then, only eight teams got to compete. Over the decades, the championship tournament continued to grow into a raging phenomenon, one that sweeps the country every March.
Canada has one member in the NCAA—Simon Fraser University.

Since 1939, this tournament—known informally as March Madness—has introduced the world to some of the greatest basketball legends of all time. Since many athletes perfect their skills in college, it is no surprise that the path to the National Basketball Association (NBA)—the professional men's basketball league—goes straight through the March Madness tournament. NBA legends Shaquille O'Neal, Earvin "Magic" Johnson, Larry Bird, Michael Jordan, Stephen Curry, and many others first made their impact in the tournament.

Though most of the NBA legends first competed in the March Madness tournament, they are among an elite class of athletes. Unfortunately, just a select few spots are available in the NBA. Scarcely 1 percent of all NCAA Division I Men's Basketball players are drafted into the NBA each year. This means that for the vast majority of players, college is the final stop in their basketball career. For them, the chance for basketball glory is now or never.

These players play with passion. It is not uncommon for one to collapse onto the floor and burst into tears at the end of a game. Sometimes it's out of happiness, sometimes disappointment, but no matter what, it's out of love for the game.

sections.title: Choosing Teams
paragraphs: The path to the championship is long and demanding. In 2016, there were 351 schools playing Men's Division I basketball. These teams are separated by region into 32 conferences of different sizes. All the teams want to play in the tournament, but only 68 will make it each year, so how are the 68 teams chosen?

Each conference has its own tournament. The winners of each of the 32 conferences automatically qualify for the March Madness tournament. The remaining 36 teams are chosen by a selection committee of athletic directors and conference commissioners. The ten-person committee considers many factors in their choices. Based on statistics, along with discussions with coaches and one another, the members vote to determine which teams will play in the tournament. This takes place a few days before the tournament on a day commonly known as Selection Sunday.
Four regions emerge with a winner to reach the Final Four. In 2016, Villanova was the South's champion—and the nation's!
The University of North Florida pep band and mascot celebrate on Selection Sunday after learning their team will play in the 2015 March Madness tournament.

sections.title: Brackets
paragraphs: March Madness is a fitting title for this historic tournament since every March, complete madness overtakes the country. It is one of the most popular sporting events in the United States, even among people who aren't sports fans. This is largely due to the fun of filling out a bracket—a grid of all the teams in the tournament. People fill out a bracket to predict who will win each game and move on to the next round of competition. A bracket shows the path a team would have to follow to reach the championship game.

Millions of people around the country fill out a bracket every year. Some base their picks on statistics. Others like the safe bet and choose the best-ranked teams, while still others love the excitement of rooting for the underdogs. Almost everyone claims to have the winning strategy, and almost everyone is trying to beat somebody else—family and friends, coworkers, or strangers online.

One of the most famous March Madness fans is former U.S. president Barack Obama. In each of his eight years as president, he appeared on a televised segment in which he filled out his own March Madness bracket.

In 2015, an eleven-year-old girl named Emilia wrote a letter to President Obama to "smack talk" his March Madness picks. Her 2015 bracket beat his. "You are a great president," she wrote, "just not the best bracket picker." President Obama wrote Emilia a letter back with a signed photo of him and his bracket that read, "Next time, I'll check with you first!"

President Obama did not have much success with his picks, however. He successfully picked the University of North Carolina Tar Heels to win the tournament in 2009, but at least while in office, he never chose another winner.

sections.title: History
paragraphs: In 1982, the NCAA started a women's March Madness basketball tournament with 32 teams. Since then it has doubled, and public interest continues to grow.

The Connecticut Huskies have been the most dominant team in women's March Madness tournament history with eleven national championships; the Tennessee Volunteers rank second with eight. Some argue that Husky Breanna Stewart may be the greatest player in women's college basketball history. From 2013 to 2016, her team won the title four years in a row. For each of those four years, she won the honor of Most Outstanding Player.

Over the course of her career, Patricia Summitt earned 1,098 wins. That's the most in NCAA basketball history. From 1974 to 2012, she coached the Tennessee Lady Volunteers. They never failed to qualify for the tournament although in the beginning, good funding failed them. When Summitt started, a donut sale paid for the players' uniforms.

The most exciting stories that come out of March Madness are "Cinderella stories." Cinderella teams are underdogs that aren't given very good odds to win. The single-elimination format of March Madness, however, means that any team can claw its way to an exciting upset victory.

Nobody gave the Villanova Wildcats much of a chance against the Georgetown Hoyas in the 1985 championship finals. Georgetown was the defending champion, having won in 1984, and was awarded the top rank that year. Meanwhile, Villanova was ranked in eighth place, and many thought their only chance of winning was by a miracle. It turned out Villanova didn't need a miracle. All they needed was to play the greatest game of their lives.

In basketball, the term field goal refers to a basket scored on any shot other than a free throw. It is worth two or three points, depending on the player's distance from the basket. Field goal percentage in basketball is how many field goals are successful out of all the field goals that the team attempts. The higher the percentage, the better!

The Wildcats shocked Georgetown by breaking the record for the highest field goal percentage in Final Four history—over 78 percent (22 of 28) in the game. This led to Villanova winning their first national championship in men's basketball with a score of 66-64.

The Kansas Jayhawks had a miraculous run in 1988. Led by star player Danny Manning, the team earned the nickname "Danny and the Miracles." This was because nobody expected them to win or even come close. The Jayhawks had just finished an eleven-loss season—the most losses that a national championship team has ever had. In 2008, Danny Manning helped Kansas capture another March Madness championship, only this time as an assistant coach.

The Middle Tennessee Blue Raiders weren't given much of a chance against the Michigan State Spartans in the 2016 opening round. The Spartans were ranked second, and many thought they should have been first; the Blue Raiders were ranked fifteenth. Most people viewed this game as a mere warm-up for Michigan State. Yet in what many call the biggest single upset in March Madness history, the Blue Raiders shocked basketball fans by defeating and eliminating Michigan State 90-81. The Blue Raiders didn't win the tournament, but they did bust a lot of people's brackets in the first round!

Everyone is looking to choose the perfect bracket. In 2014, billionaire Warren Buffett and Quicken Loans offered $1 billion to anyone who chose the perfect bracket, but no one could. This is not surprising. The odds of picking every game correctly are between 1 in 5 billion and 1 in 135 billion. As far as we know, nobody has ever chosen the perfect bracket.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: Every March, millions of people take in the excitement of Cinderella teams, rising stars, incredible athletic feats, and game-winning shots at the buzzer. Dreams will either come true or fade away forever. This is passion. This is March Madness. Good luck with your bracket!
Duke rejoices after beating Wisconsin in the 2015 men's championship game. Maryland beats Louisville in 2014 to advance to the women's Final Four.

vocabulary.definition: a grid that pairs opponents in a tournament
vocabulary.sentence: This is largely due to the fun of filling out a bracket—a grid of all the teams in the tournament.

options: a television show about college basketball
a grid that pairs opponents in a tournament
a list of the best-ranked teams in the country
a type of trophy given to the champion

vocabulary.definition: more important, effective, or powerful than others
vocabulary.sentence: The Connecticut Huskies have been the most dominant team in women's March Madness tournament history with eleven national championships.

options: more important, effective, or powerful than others
new and unknown to most people
likely to lose in the first round
popular among casual sports fans

vocabulary.definition: a series of games in which a single loss prevents the loser from moving on in the competition
vocabulary.sentence: With sixty-eight teams playing in a single-elimination tournament, anything can happen.

options: a series of games in which only the top-ranked teams are allowed to participate
a series of games where one loss causes a team to be removed from the competition
a series of games in which teams may lose multiple times but still compete
a series of games in which fans help determine which teams advance

vocabulary.definition: the expected losers in a struggle or contest
vocabulary.sentence: Underdogs NC State are playing the game of their lives, and with mere seconds left the score is tied 52-52.

options: the teams expected to win the championship easily
the teams that have won the most tournaments
the expected losers in a struggle or contest
the youngest players on each team

vocabulary.definition: the quality of being impossible to know ahead of time
vocabulary.sentence: This is what March Madness is all about: excitement, high stakes, and unpredictability.

options: the quality of being impossible to know ahead of time
the excitement of watching a close basketball game
the ability to plan ahead for a tournament
the skill needed to fill out a winning bracket

vocabulary.definition: unexpected results in sports games or other competitions
vocabulary.sentence: It has just witnessed one of the biggest and most exciting upsets in college basketball history.

options: games that end in a tie score
expected results when top teams play
blowouts where one team dominates
unexpected results in sports games or other competitions

vocabulary.sentence: The crowd erupts in chaos and celebration.

options: confusion; lack of order
a quiet and peaceful moment
a carefully planned celebration
a type of defensive play in basketball

vocabulary.definition: selected out of a group to play on a professional sports team
vocabulary.sentence: Scarcely 1 percent of all NCAA Division I Men's Basketball players are drafted into the NBA each year.

quiz.prompt: When a player is "drafted," they are:
options: rejected from playing professional sports
selected out of a group to play on a professional sports team
traded to a different college team
asked to coach a younger team

vocabulary.definition: famous people who are known for a special talent or achievement
vocabulary.sentence: Since 1939, this tournament—known informally as March Madness—has introduced the world to some of the greatest basketball legends of all time.

options: unknown players trying out for a team
coaches who train young athletes
famous people who are known for a special talent or achievement
fans who follow a sport for many years

vocabulary.definition: controls the behavior of something so it works right or obeys laws or rules
vocabulary.sentence: The National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) organizes, coordinates, and regulates college and university sports in the United States.

options: ignore the rules of a game
destroy something completely
control the behavior of something so it works right or obeys laws or rules
create a brand new sport from scratch

vocabulary.definition: numerical data that is collected, organized, and studied
vocabulary.sentence: Based on statistics, along with discussions with coaches and one another, the members vote to determine which teams will play in the tournament.

options: personal opinions about which team is best
numerical data that is collected, organized, and studied
feelings that fans have about a game
predictions made by sports reporters

vocabulary.definition: a careful plan for solving a problem or achieving a goal
vocabulary.sentence: Almost everyone claims to have the winning strategy, and almost everyone is trying to beat somebody else.

options: a careful plan for solving a problem or achieving a goal
an accident that happens during a game
a coincidence that leads to a surprise win
a lucky guess about the outcome

questions.question: What is March Madness?
questions.evidence: This is what March Madness is all about: excitement, high stakes, and unpredictability. With sixty-eight teams playing in a single-elimination tournament, anything can happen.
choices: A professional basketball league in the United States
A college basketball tournament with 68 teams
A women's basketball championship with 32 teams
An international basketball competition held every year
questions.explanation: March Madness is the NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Tournament—a single-elimination tournament with 68 college teams where anything can happen.

questions.question: What is the main idea of the section "How the Madness Works"?
questions.evidence: The path to the championship is long and demanding. In 2016, there were 351 schools playing Men's Division I basketball. These teams are separated by region into 32 conferences of different sizes. All the teams want to play in the tournament, but only 68 will make it each year, so how are the 68 teams chosen?
choices: The Final Four teams include one team from each region that compete for the championship title.
In 2016, Villanova was the South's champion and went on to win the national championship.
The path to the championship in the March Madness tournament is long and demanding.
In 2016, there were 351 schools playing Men's Division I basketball.
questions.explanation: The section 'How the Madness Works' explains how 68 teams are chosen from 351 schools through conference tournaments and a selection committee. The main idea is that the path is long and demanding.

questions.question: In what year did the NCAA start a women's March Madness basketball tournament?
questions.evidence: In 1982, the NCAA started a women's March Madness basketball tournament with 32 teams.
questions.explanation: The NCAA started the women's March Madness basketball tournament in 1982 with 32 teams. It has since doubled in size.

questions.question: Which of the following is an opinion?
questions.evidence: March Madness is a fitting title for this historic tournament since every March, complete madness overtakes the country. It is one of the most popular sporting events in the United States.
choices: March Madness is the most exciting sporting event of the year to watch on television.
Former president Barack Obama appeared on a televised segment filling out his bracket.
On March 17, 1939, the NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Tournament began.
From 2013 to 2016, Breanna Stewart's team won the championship four years in a row.
questions.explanation: Saying March Madness is 'the most exciting sporting event' is an opinion—it's what someone believes, not a proven fact. The other choices can all be verified as true.

questions.question: What are Cinderella teams?
questions.evidence: Cinderella teams are underdogs that aren't given very good odds to win. The single-elimination format of March Madness, however, means that any team can claw its way to an exciting upset victory.
choices: teams that are out of the tournament if they lose one game
teams that are not given good odds to win but win anyway
teams that are given good odds to win the tournament
teams that have the best players in the league
questions.explanation: Cinderella teams are underdogs—teams that aren't expected to win but surprise everyone by winning anyway, just like Cinderella in the fairy tale.

questions.question: What is one effect of the March Madness tournament?
questions.evidence: Since 1939, this tournament—known informally as March Madness—has introduced the world to some of the greatest basketball legends of all time.
choices: Fewer people are interested in watching sports than before.
More people are attempting to play basketball in college.
College basketball players are earning more money each year.
The world has been introduced to some of the greatest basketball players.
questions.explanation: Since 1939, the tournament has introduced the world to basketball legends like Michael Jordan, Magic Johnson, Larry Bird, and Stephen Curry. This is a direct effect of the tournament.

questions.question: What is the effect of a team being chosen by the ten-person committee on Selection Sunday?
questions.evidence: The remaining 36 teams are chosen by a selection committee of athletic directors and conference commissioners. Based on statistics, along with discussions with coaches and one another, the members vote to determine which teams will play in the tournament.
choices: It gets to play another team to try to win a spot in the tournament.
It gets to play in the March Madness tournament.
It is not included in the March Madness tournament.
It must convince the committee that it should be allowed to play.
questions.explanation: When the selection committee chooses a team on Selection Sunday, that team earns a spot in the March Madness tournament. The 32 conference champions qualify automatically; the committee selects the remaining 36.

questions.question: How do you think the author feels about college basketball players?
questions.evidence: These players play with passion. It is not uncommon for one to collapse onto the floor and burst into tears at the end of a game. Sometimes it's out of happiness, sometimes disappointment, but no matter what, it's out of love for the game.
choices: The author believes they get too carried away with excitement.
The author believes the athletes make too much money playing.
The author believes they are hardworking and passionate.
The author believes they are just as skilled as other college athletes.
questions.explanation: The author describes players collapsing in tears 'out of love for the game' and playing 'with passion.' This shows the author sees them as deeply passionate and dedicated to basketball.

questions.question: What is the author's point of view on March Madness?
questions.evidence: Every March, millions of people take in the excitement of Cinderella teams, rising stars, incredible athletic feats, and game-winning shots at the buzzer. Dreams will either come true or fade away forever. This is passion. This is March Madness.
choices: The author believes March Madness has become too big, and fewer teams should participate.
The author believes March Madness is just as interesting as any other sporting event.
The author believes March Madness is an exciting time when anything can happen.
The author believes March Madness does not give each team a fair opportunity to win.
questions.explanation: The author's language throughout the book shows excitement and positivity—words like 'passion,' 'excitement,' and 'anything can happen.' The author clearly views March Madness as an thrilling, exciting event.

questions.question: Unexpected results in sports games or other competitions are called:
questions.evidence: Almost everyone claims to have the winning strategy, and almost everyone is trying to beat somebody else—family and friends, coworkers, or strangers online.
choices: game statistics
official regulations
team strategies
surprising upsets
questions.explanation: Upsets are unexpected results in sports. When an underdog team beats a favored team, it's called an upset. March Madness is famous for its upsets!

questions.question: Why do you think the author chose to start the book by describing the 1983 national championship game between NC State and the Houston Cougars? How does this opening connect to the rest of the book?
questions.explanation: The author starts with the 1983 championship game to hook the reader with an exciting, dramatic moment. NC State was an underdog that pulled off one of the biggest upsets in history—this shows how 'anything can happen' in March Madness. It connects to the rest of the book because unpredictability, upsets, and Cinderella stories are central themes throughout.
opinionPrompts: Why might the author start with a dramatic story instead of facts about the tournament?
How does the 1983 NC State game show the 'madness' of March Madness?
What evidence from the text supports the idea that the author wants to excite the reader?

answerKey.Inform: The author provides facts about the history, structure, and teams of March Madness.
answerKey.Persuade: The author shows a positive view of the tournament's excitement and passion.

evidence: The author explains how 68 teams are chosen from 351 schools through conferences and a selection committee.
The author provides historical facts: the tournament began in 1939 with eight teams.
The author describes the bracket system and how the single-elimination format works.
While the book is engaging, the author's main goal is not to entertain with a fictional story.
The text presents real facts and events, not a made-up adventure.
The author uses exciting language like 'complete madness overtakes the country' to show a positive view.
The author highlights the passion of players and the thrill of Cinderella stories.
The closing line 'This is passion. This is March Madness.' shows enthusiasm.

summaries: The NCAA organizes college sports in the United States. Since 1939, the March Madness tournament has grown from 8 teams to 68 teams and introduced the world to basketball legends. Most players never reach the NBA, so college is their final chance at glory.

The March Madness tournament began in 1939 and has grown into one of the most exciting sporting events in the United States. The NCAA regulates college sports and selects 68 teams each year to compete. Since few players make it to the NBA, every game could be a player's last.

March Madness is a single-elimination tournament where 68 college basketball teams compete each year. The NCAA regulates the tournament, which has been running since 1939. Many NBA legends got their start in March Madness, but only about 1% of players are drafted into the NBA.

From 351 Division I schools, 68 teams are chosen for the tournament. Conference winners qualify automatically, and a selection committee picks the remaining 36 teams based on statistics and discussions on Selection Sunday.

The path to the tournament is demanding. Each of the 32 conferences holds its own tournament, and the winner advances automatically. A ten-person committee then selects the remaining 36 teams after careful review.

Choosing the 68 teams for March Madness is a long process. The 32 conference champions earn automatic spots, while a selection committee uses statistics and expert discussions to choose the remaining 36 teams on Selection Sunday.

March Madness is one of the most popular sporting events in the U.S., even for non-fans, because of the fun of filling out brackets. Millions of people use different strategies to predict winners. Even President Obama participated by filling out his bracket on television each year.

Brackets are grids that show all tournament matchups. Millions of people fill them out every year—some use statistics, some pick favorites, and some root for underdogs. President Obama was a famous bracket picker, though he didn't have much success.

Every year, millions of Americans fill out brackets to predict March Madness winners. Some use careful strategies based on statistics, while others simply root for underdogs. The bracket challenge is so popular that even the U.S. president participates every year.

The women's tournament began in 1982 and has grown significantly, with the Connecticut Huskies and Tennessee Volunteers as the most dominant programs. The men's tournament has produced famous Cinderella stories, like Villanova's 1985 upset, Kansas's 1988 run, and Middle Tennessee's 2016 shocker.

March Madness history is full of exciting upsets and legendary performances. The women's tournament started in 1982 with 32 teams and has doubled. The men's tournament has produced unforgettable Cinderella stories where underdogs defeated heavily favored teams.

Both men's and women's March Madness tournaments have rich histories of upsets and achievements. The women's game has grown from 32 teams to 64, with dominant programs like Connecticut. The men's game has seen underdogs like Villanova, Kansas, and Middle Tennessee pull off stunning upsets.

Every March, millions of people enjoy the excitement of Cinderella teams, rising stars, and incredible plays. The tournament's unpredictability makes it thrilling—dreams come true or fade away. This is the passion of March Madness.

March Madness captivates millions every year with its combination of underdog stories, amazing athletic performances, and last-second shots. The single-elimination format means anything can happen, making the tournament one of the most exciting events in sports.

The conclusion celebrates what makes March Madness special: the passion of players, the thrill of the unexpected, and the dreams that come alive every March. Whether you fill out a bracket or just watch the games, the tournament brings excitement to millions.

grammar.title: Exclamation Marks
grammar.explanation: An exclamation mark (!) is used at the end of a sentence to show strong emotion, excitement, surprise, or emphasis. Use it when a sentence expresses a feeling that is too strong for just a period.
rules: Use an exclamation mark after a sentence that shows strong emotion or excitement.
Use an exclamation mark for commands given with strong feeling: 'Stop right there!'
Do not use an exclamation mark for calm, factual statements—use a period instead.
Never use both a period and an exclamation mark at the end of the same sentence.

exercises.sentence: In 2016, Villanova was the South's champion—and the nation's!
answers: The sentence correctly uses an exclamation mark to show excitement about Villanova's championship win.
exercises.hint: Does this sentence express strong emotion or excitement?

exercises.sentence: The crowd erupts in chaos and celebration.
answers: This sentence describes an exciting moment but uses a period. It could use an exclamation mark to show the strong excitement.
exercises.hint: Is this sentence describing a calm or exciting moment?

exercises.sentence: The Blue Raiders didn't win the tournament, but they did bust a lot of people's brackets in the first round!
answers: The sentence correctly uses an exclamation mark to show the surprising, exciting nature of the upset.
exercises.hint: Does this sentence express surprise or strong feeling?

exercises.question: Which sentence should end with an exclamation mark?
options: The tournament has 68 teams
On March 17, 1939, the NCAA Division I Men's Basketball Tournament began its historic run
What an incredible upset that was
The Connecticut Huskies have won eleven national championships
exercises.explanation: 'What an incredible upset that was' expresses strong excitement and surprise, so it should end with an exclamation mark: 'What an incredible upset that was!'

exercises.question: Which sentence uses correct punctuation?
options: This is passion This is March Madness.
This is passion! This is March Madness!
This is passion, This is March Madness,
This is passion; This is March Madness;
exercises.explanation: Each short, emphatic sentence expresses strong emotion and excitement. Exclamation marks are the correct choice: 'This is passion! This is March Madness!'

exercises.sentence: The crowd erupts in chaos and celebration ___
exercises.explanation: The crowd erupting in chaos and celebration shows strong excitement, so an exclamation mark is appropriate.

exercises.sentence: This is passion ___ This is March Madness ___ Good luck with your bracket ___
exercises.explanation: All three sentences express strong emotion and excitement. Each should end with an exclamation mark.

exercises.sentence: The Blue Raiders shocked basketball fans by defeating Michigan State 90-81 ___
exercises.explanation: This was a huge upset that shocked fans, so an exclamation mark shows the excitement of the moment.

nowOrNever.title: Chaos
nowOrNever.content: Chaos means confusion or complete lack of order. When the crowd 'erupts in chaos,' everyone is jumping, screaming, and celebrating at once—total excitement!

nowOrNever.title: Underdogs & Upsets
nowOrNever.content: Underdogs are the teams expected to LOSE. An upset is when an underdog WINS! NC State was an underdog in 1983—they were expected to lose, but they won!

nowOrNever.title: Regulates
nowOrNever.content: The NCAA REGULATES (controls) college sports. They make the rules and make sure everyone follows them. Think: regulate = rules!

nowOrNever.title: Exclamation Marks
nowOrNever.content: Exclamation marks (!) show STRONG emotion. The author writes: 'This is what March Madness is all about!' The excitement is too big for just a period!

nowOrNever.title: The 1983 Upset
nowOrNever.content: The author opens with the 1983 NC State vs. Houston game. Why? It shows that in March Madness, UNDERDOGS can win and ANYTHING can happen! This sets up the whole book's theme.

choosingTeams.title: Statistics
choosingTeams.content: Statistics (or 'stats') are numbers that are collected and studied. The selection committee uses STATISTICS to decide which teams get into the tournament!

choosingTeams.title: How Teams Are Chosen
choosingTeams.content: 32 conference winners → automatically in! The other 36 teams → chosen by a 10-person committee using statistics. That's how 68 teams are selected from 351 schools!

choosingTeams.title: Selection Sunday
choosingTeams.content: Selection Sunday is when the committee announces which teams made it. Imagine finding out your team gets to play in the tournament—that's why the pep band celebrates!

brackets.title: Bracket & Strategy
brackets.content: A BRACKET is a grid showing all tournament matchups. People use different STRATEGIES (plans) to fill them out—some use statistics, some pick favorites, and some root for underdogs!

brackets.title: The $1 Billion Challenge
brackets.content: In 2014, Warren Buffett offered $1 BILLION for a perfect bracket. Nobody won! The odds of picking every game are between 1 in 5 billion and 1 in 135 billion!

brackets.title: Obama's Bracket
brackets.content: President Obama filled out his bracket on TV every year. But an 11-year-old girl named Emilia beat his! She wrote: 'You are a great president, just not the best bracket picker.'

history.title: Dominant
history.content: Dominant means MORE POWERFUL than others. The Connecticut Huskies are the most DOMINANT women's team with 11 championships! Breanna Stewart won 4 in a row!

history.title: Legends
history.content: Legends are FAMOUS people known for special achievements. NBA legends like Michael Jordan, Magic Johnson, and Larry Bird all played in March Madness first!

history.title: Drafted
history.content: When a player is DRAFTED, they are SELECTED to play on a professional team. But only about 1% of NCAA players are drafted into the NBA each year!

history.title: Cinderella Stories
history.content: Cinderella teams = underdogs who WIN! Like Villanova in 1985 (ranked 8th!) beating Georgetown, or Middle Tennessee in 2016 (ranked 15th!) beating #2 Michigan State!

history.title: Women's Tournament Growth
history.content: The women's tournament started in 1982 with 32 teams and has DOUBLED since then. Patricia Summitt coached for 38 years and earned 1,098 wins—the most in NCAA history!

conclusion.title: Author's Point of View
conclusion.content: The author's POSITIVE view is clear: 'This is passion. This is March Madness!' The author uses exciting words throughout to show how thrilling the tournament is.

conclusion.title: Exclamation Marks in the Conclusion
conclusion.content: The conclusion uses exclamation marks to show excitement: 'Good luck with your bracket!' The author can barely contain the enthusiasm for March Madness!

wordWork.title: Alphabetical Order
wordWork.instructions: Put the 12 Words to Know in alphabetical order.
